sentences.text: Sure.
As I best recall, yes.
And of course, we didn't bring any utensils.
I'm sure everybody had the toothbrushes and combs and things like that.
But utensils-- so we had these tin bowls--
--large spoon, and a cup that was issued-- that was issued to us.
Well, my-- they-- they-- they could spend together.
I mean, people would meet, and they would talk.
But primarily, people were depressed, in fear, not knowing what to do.
I mean, if you had a job, something to do at least, you know.
If ?] your mind was wandering every minute, what's going to happen to me?
What's going to be?
So people were very, very depressed.
My father and mother didn't have that much time together because my father was working.
I mean, you know, with a thousand-some-odd people-- I don't know how many of them were children.
Always somebody was sick, and people needed to be at the hospital.
I really don't know.
I mean, some people obviously had to get sick.
I mean, people get sick, you know, under normal circumstances.
And being deprived of food and proper medication, I'm sure people got sick.
I'm sure we almost had one or two deaths, also.
I mean, just [INAUDIBLE], you know.
I-- but I really can't tell you for sure.
Not because of specific abuse.
Well, there were people who were craftsmen.
And craftspeople repaired shoes, you know, who were able to-- I mean, the craftspeople generally bring their tools with them, you know, just like people-- craftspeople just do-- do that.
And of course, those people were mending shoes, and women were, of course, fixing hems in clothing and shortening.
And people were selling-- selling clothing that were-- made the lining for warm-up jackets or coats.
My father, having been a pediatrician in a small town, which was mainly in a agrarian society, a lot of times got paid not in money, but in kind.
And he has this leather vest.
I guess it was a sheepskin, got-- it was very well embroidered and what have you and nicely done.
Some Hungarian peasant bartered him with that.
That probably saved his life.
I mean, he wore it under his clothes, and I still have it.
I still have it.
It's an old, old leather vest.
And then subsequently my mother knitted some sleeves to it and I wore it, but I took the sleeves off.
I still have it as a souvenir.
Right.
We play.
We try to learn some Hebrew songs.
I said we learned how to dance the hora.
We were planning to go to Palestine.
You know, it's [? aliyah. ?] We're going to the-- the land, the Promised Land.
And we learned songs, and we sang.
We played.
Really, we played.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I look back-- of course, it was no fun being there.
But I look back, it's the same as-- as-- as the children in the other ghettos.
We're not starving.
I mean, for children, young children, you know, it's boring.
You're not going to school.
You just-- you have to have something to do.
And children are very, very resilient in finding games to play.
And so we played games.
We played engineer.
We played, like I said, we played soccer, the boys.
And-- but I don't remember playing with my sister.
I just remember playing with the boys and trying to learn a little bit of Polish from those orphans that were with us.
And you know, it was a very long day.
And you get up, and you wait for-- we were exhausted by the time the appell was over, and you haven't done anything.
And of course, you felt very bad for the people next door who were working.
They were building.
They were doing.
They were constructing all the time for more people to come.
No, I don't think they were Jews.
[INAUDIBLE] The Dutch were Jews, next to us.
Did you want to ask me anything, because there's something that I--
--meant to say when I-- I planned this-- this out, and I just-- I'm not saying it the right way.
If you recall, I mentioned that people would write their names or carve their names into the beds, the triple-decker beds.
I mean, fate is-- truth is stranger than fiction.
Three days or four days after we left, they were moving the people out of the East.
And they brought my mother's mother and my father's mother to the very same compound in Bergen-Belsen where we were.
And of course, my father's mother was in the B barracks, because her name was Berger.
In exact same place where we were.
And we knew we were going to Switzerland.
So we left for Switzerland, and she said, I know that my children are safe, and now I can die in peace.
And she died in Bergen-Belsen.
That's my-- my father's mother, my paternal grandmother.
My maternal grandmother, who was there also, she survived the war, and she went back to Yugoslavia and subsequently to Israel.
But you know, the thing about putting your name down, and--
I did not.
My mother had put down, as-- as everybody else, you know, did.
Who knows--
She had just written her name.
Yeah.
Because, you know, somehow, yes, did you see-- well, we know that on such-and-such that so-and-so was still alive because of the date and things.
And that's how people would know that people lived or were here or passed through.
And later on, when people and families were reunited, this was one of the ways that stories got around.
Yes.
We were there-- well, we were-- well, much longer.
We were in Caux until the end of the war.
After that, we went to a place called Clarens, C-L-A-R-E-N-S, which is right down on the Lake Geneva.
It's between a town of of Vevey and Montreux.
And we were at a place called Maribeau, M-A-R-I-B-E-A-U.
Again, my father was a physician.
Now, stranger than that is what I mentioned about my grandparents.
My wife's family was with us in the very same concentration camp at the very same time.
Her-- her brothers?
My wife.
And her mother and father.
And they were with us also not only in Caux, but they also were with us in the other part of Switzerland.
When the war was over and Tito asked for people to come back, my father said, hell no.
He said, I don't like fascism.
I don't like communism.
I don't like any -isms.
I'm a free man who likes to have free thought.
And I survived this war not to go back to something.
So when Tito asked to go back, my dad just refused to go back.
My father-in-law, on the other hand, took his family and went back to Yugoslavia.
[INAUDIBLE].
So we then were there during the summer of '40, part of the summer of '40.
I'm sorry, I'm getting the dates mixed up.
It was in '45 or '46.
Maybe it was late '45. '46 we were-- early '46 we were sent to another place where-- called Aigle, A-I-G-L-E, and we were the only Jews there.
It was Russian workers and their families, who had been, you know, forced laborers in Germany who were-- and brought to Switzerland.
And we were there.
We were the only Jews-- my dad and my mother, my sister, and I.
And we got along very well with the administrative staff, the Swiss officers.
And to this date, I mean, just the last year the last one died.
We were constantly in contact with them.
Every New Year and holidays that we would-- we kept in touch with them.
I started learning French.
No, please.
In the first--
In Caux, yes.
Clarens.
First is Clarens.
A group, a group.
Not a lot.
Because they were moving-- trying to move people around.
The war was over now.
The Swiss government.
You know, you were DP.
You were a displaced person.
And you were stateless, displaced person with no passports.
Well, all the places were similar, all the same.
Now, after some time, we went to a place in a town called Teritet, T-E-R-I-T-E-T, also in the Swiss part, also not far from Montreux.
And here we went to a home, which also had been, once upon a time, a hotel.
We were under the Swiss administration, but of course there were no cops.
There were no police, no army.
The war was over.
And they received money from the Red Cross.
And some people were given permission to live there and work in the economy, and the others were forced to live there.
My sister and I were, as the other children were, allowed to go to the local school.
So it's my first time I'm going to school was in 1946 in French.
'46, I was nine.
Actually, it was-- I'm sorry.
It must have been January of-- or the the end of January '46 I was eight.
I was eight, [INAUDIBLE] the very beginning [INAUDIBLE] or December of '45.
And I went to school, and it was a two-room school, first and second grade one room, third, fourth, and fifth grade in the other room.
It was a nice school.
And I have to tell you, they took us in.
The children were so nice and so friendly.
They took us in.
And we still have friends from those days we'd gone to school with, and we keep in contact, especially my sister.
And we came and went, and we're fine.
And we [INAUDIBLE].
My dad, of course, worked, not in the economy, but worked as a physician at the place.
Now, the Joint Distribution Agency and the Red Cross began publishing lists of people who survived the war.
And a man named Mr.
Havas, H-A-V-A-S, also his first name, just like my dad's, Imre, I-M-R-E, was looking through the list, and he sees IM Berger, wife and two children.
So he said, is this my dear friend and schoolmate, or is it somebody with the same initials?
What am I going to do?
I am going to send a check, and I think it was for $50, drawn on the Irving Trust Company at 225 Broadway.
I will sign my name.
In those days, you didn't have the person's name printed on the-- on the check.
So he said, if this is my former roommate, he will recognize my signature.
He will be intelligent enough to find a way to get in touch with me.
And sure enough--
--it was us.
It was him.
My dad writes-- wrote a letter, Dear Mr.
Irving, not knowing what the Irving Trust Company was.
And the banks were a little different.
When Mr.
Havas walked into that branch, people say, good morning, Mr.
Havas.
You know, it's like people knew who you were in those days.
People that worked in the bank didn't work there three weeks, and they were sent out to an ATM machine.
And this correspondence was started.
And Mr.
Havas had two brothers, and they were raised by the grandparents.
They were orphan-- raised by the grandparents and were very, very close friends of my father, dear friends of my father.
And one of them was in the motion picture business.
And he was the European rep for RKO, and he was stationed in Paris.
So of course, contacts were-- and he got in contact with us.
He came to Switzerland.
You know, he has an American passport.
He came to Switzerland to see us where we were living in Teritet.
His younger brother, Mike, yes.
Pardon me?
Who also--
Oh, that was good-- I mean, they [INAUDIBLE] came from [INAUDIBLE], three-- these three brothers came from [INAUDIBLE], I mean, the same town.
Mr.
Havas, if you had ever seen the movie he took that last boat, that last plane.
He just got out just in time, and he got here just in time to sort of-- to save himself.
And his two other brothers were-- one was working for Disney and for RKO, so he was an American citizen.
And the third one, Eugene, he was a financial advisor to FDR.
So he had been here many, many, many years before the older-- the oldest.
And of course, contacts were made.
And it was almost impossible to come to the United States then.
I mean, the-- the quota for Hungarian or Yugoslav Jews were almost nil, and it was very difficult.
So my dad decided that he had had his fill of Europe.
He was a Zionist.
And he could not get, obviously, a-- a piece of paper from the British that we could go to Palestine.
I assume you-- you know that, that  it was such terrific restriction on Jewish immigration to Palestine.
So didn't want to remain a displaced person.
Did not want to go back.
We're ready come to the New World, and you couldn't come to the United States.
However, they were very desperate for physicians in Bolivia.
So we were going to go to Bolivia and start learning Spanish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
About a month before we were supposed to go to Bolivia, we get an affidavit that, based upon a preference quota for physicians, we could come to the United States.
So now shift gears.
I have to now learn English.
So my father had to learn English.
And my sister and I took a few lessons in English.
And I remember the first thing I learned in English was about Beatrix Potter tales.
In 1947, in June, we left Switzerland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We traveled to Paris, and we spent a few days in Paris with-- and we stayed as a guest of Mr.
Havas, the one was in the film business.
Then we went to London and stayed with one of my father's classmates from school, who was a dentist and was living in the West End.
And then we took a ship called the a ship on the Polish registry going under the Belgian flag, and we came to the United States.
It took eight days to cross the Atlantic.
I thought I was on such a huge ship.
When we docked next to the we looked like a tugboat compared to her.
I mean, it was a terribly, terribly small ship in comparison.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a wonderful trip.
Food?
I hadn't had that much food and never plentiful food ever in my life, as I recalled.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We arrived to New York on a very steamy, muggy, horrible, typical New York summer day on July 16, 1947, and we arrived very early in the morning.
Coming from Switzerland, we did not have to go through-- I don't even know if Ellis Island was still operating then.
We did not have to go to Ellis Island.
The-- the people from the Department of Health came on board, immigration people came on board.
Took quite a while to get off.
But the interesting thing was that I saw the West Side Highway and so many automobiles.
I mean, you know, I was a seven-year-old kid.
And in Europe, there were very, very few cars.
There was very little fuel.
And the number of automobiles was just-- just mind-boggling.
And it looked like cockroaches or mice coming down the West Side Highway in the early, early, early part of the morning.
And, of course, there was a terrible thunderstorm and rain, so it was overcast for a long time.
So even while the day had begun, not only at 6:00 or 7:00, but about 8, 9 o'clock in the morning, the height of rush hour, it was amazing.
We got off the boat finally.
And everybody wore hats in those days.
So Mr.
Havas was there waiting for us with a straw hat.
Another woman, a Mrs.

Hecht, H-E-C-H-T, who was in a-- who was in the same barrack with us on top, on the third barrack-- on the third bed and high up, who had come to the United States early, she was there waiting for us, one of my mother's cousins, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were waiting.
And we're very hungry, because it had been quite some time.
And the only way I could speak with Mr.
Havas was in Hungarian.
So I start speaking Hungarian to him about how hungry I was.
And all of a sudden, here's this man, a vendor, who's selling, in Hungarian said, how would you like to have a ham sandwich?
I mean, I can't believe this.
What a wonderful country.
Everybody speaks Hungarian here.
Mr.
Havas spoke Hungarian, and this vendor spoke Hungarian.
And-- Were you really excited when you were coming?
Ah, it was-- I mean, it was a-- you know, I was so seasick for two days, but after that it was just the most marvelous thing.
The crossing the-- the Atlantic was wonderful.
And of course, seeing the Statue of Liberty, it didn't mean anything to me.
I was a seven-year-old kid.
Didn't mean any-- now it means a lot to me.
But [? personally, ?] it didn't mean-- the buildings.
I mean, I had seen the film with Joel Grey, I thought there were cowboys.
I mean, really, you know, there were cowboys and, you know, this is nowadays, and there were cowboys and [? people, ?] horses.
I also saw other films thinking that all of the United States was noontime down on Wall Street area, with thousands of people walking.
These were the preceptions that I had coming to the United States, obviously.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we moved to Queens, one and two-family-- one and two-story buildings.
You know, totally, totally, totally different when we came here.
We stayed in the Bronx for three days, the first three days with some friends.
And then we moved to Queens in three days after we'd been here.
Then, of course, I started school again, and this time I started in English.
Right.
I had a lot of problems.
In those days, young boys didn't wear slacks.
Wore shorts-- wore shorts in the summertime, wore shorts in the wintertime.
And of course, only sissies wear shorts here, or so it seems in 1947.
So I was in a fight every day for the first couple months that I was in school.
But by that time, I spoke pretty good English quite well, because we came in July, and school started in September.
And in two months, you know, I managed to get a working vocabulary and speak the language relatively well.
Oh, yes, it was, it was.
My father-- well, firstly, my father, being a professional, he had to take a-- learn English.
There was a special English for-- examination you had to pass as a foreign professional.
Once you pass that before, then you could first study and take an examination for your specialty, if you were a physician or dentist or attorney, what have you.
It was very, very difficult for them.
My mother had been a prima donna, the daughter of very, very wealthy, as I told you, very, very wealthy parents.
My father was, by the time he got married, was a rather successful pediatrician.
And I told you we had three in help.
So she didn't-- she didn't boil water.
But when we were in Switzerland, Ort, O-R-T, taught her how to cook, how to sew, how to do all these things.
And here he is-- she was the prima donna.
For three cents a buttonhole she was making, but she went to work making buttonholes in a-- in a sweatshop when we first came here in 1947.
Because your father wasn't able to work--
My father--
Well, my father went to study.
Once he passed his test, then he worked as an intern. $50 a month they paid.
Even then it was very little money.
And slowly, slowly [INAUDIBLE].
My father never wanted to go back.
We gave up our Yugoslav citizenship, and we became American citizens.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in contradistinction to many of our friends and co-commiserators, my dad never wanted anything from the Germans.
So we got our lives, you know, whereas a lot of people-- we lost a lot, an awful lot, but never asked for any repatriation or anything like that.
And let's put our past behind.
Let's not forget, and let's go forward.
You said your--
We are now Americans, and we're going to be Americans.
And we're going to live as Americans.
We're going to be Jews and proud Jews, but we're going to be Americans with a central European heritage.
He always was a Zionist.
Now, my mother did not want to go and live in Palestine.
In 1934, they went to Palestine on their honeymoon.
At the same time, her father was exhibiting at the Near East Fair in the fairground, which is now still the fairgrounds in-- in Tel Aviv.
And I mean, it was just sand, not dirt, sand.
It was just beach and sand and heat and the flies and-- and my mother said no.
She's never going to live there in no-- no way, shape, or form.
That was in '34.
By '47, she had two children, and we came here because it was now impossible to go.
And she said, well, we're going to make our life over here.
Now, the rest of our family that survived, her two brothers, my aunt , and my maternal grandmother, the first chance that Tito let the Jews go from Yugoslavia to Israel, they immigrated to Israel.
My wife's another story.
My wife also went to Israel with her family.
They went back to Yugoslavia and-- because they were searching, I guess, for her grandparents.
And also, her father wanted to go back, and he was a pharmacist.
They went back.
And then in '48, they went on the first ship that allowed to take children, because she had a young brother who was just born.
And so on the first ship that took children, they went, and they went to Israel.
And although we had known each other as children, of course she didn't remember me.
She was two years old, and I was seven.
And I-- of course I didn't remember her.
But when I was in Israel for the first time in 1965, I was driving in an automobile with my mother and one of her cousins.
And I said, you know, I know that couple.
And it was my future father-in-law and his wife.
You know, I-- I had remembered them.
And yes, these are the [INAUDIBLE] et cetera, et cetera, et cetera.
And nothing was said and done.
And then we were-- they invited us to come over for coffee and some cake.
We went to the house.
And of course, they had known each other very, very well.
And I saw a picture of my wife, who was working for El Al at the time.
And she was-- you could not fly through the Sinai-- or the Suez or what have you.
So they had to go to Iran and take a base in East Africa, because they were flying to South Africa.
So my wife, they would-- they would send you for six weeks there.
So she was stationed six weeks in-- in Kenya at the time.
And I said, well, why don't you come, you know, jokingly, come visit when she comes to New York?
And so she flies there.
Why don't you come look me up?
She came and looked me up, and here we are, 30 years, husband and wife.
Wow.
That's an amazing story.
Mm-hmm.
